subject: Mandatory arbitration and class-action waivers — AT&T Mobility v. Concepcion (2011) and Epic Systems v. Lewis (2018)
central_question: Every time you click 'I agree,' you may be signing away your right to ever take a company to court — or to band together with others who were harmed. How did the fine print get that power?
viewer_promise: By the end, the viewer understands how a dispute over about thirty dollars made arbitration clauses with class-action waivers enforceable across American consumer and employment contracts — and the genuine debate over whether that is good or bad.
surprise: The most common contract in your life — the one you agree to without reading — can quietly take away your right to sue and your right to join a class action, and the Supreme Court said companies can do exactly that.
stakes: Whether you can take a company to court, or join others, when a product or employer wrongs you in a small but widespread way.
target_audience: English-speaking US general audience: consumers, workers, anyone who clicks 'I agree'
demand_evidence: 'The fine print that took away your right to sue' is a highly relatable, self-relevant hidden-system story; arbitration clauses touch nearly every consumer and worker.
Recognizable, evergreen, and advertiser-safe if kept neutral.
competition: Coverage is split between consumer-advocacy and business framings; few give a neutral 'what the Court held + both sides' explainer.
differentiation: Present-tense, self-relevant (the 'I agree' you just clicked); strictly neutral, both-sides; part of the 'what you can SAY / what you sign away' cluster.
research_feasibility: High: opinions are public domain (563 U.S. 333; 584 U.S. 497); Cornell LII, Oyez, Justia document the record.
visual_feasibility: High: a finger tapping 'I Agree'; scrolling fine print; one tiny claim vs a crowd of many; a courthouse door closing and an 'arbitration' door opening.
risk_notes: Decided landmark cases (2011, 2018); opinions public domain - advertiser-friendly.
POLICY DEBATE — keep strictly neutral and balanced. 'Forced arbitration' is a contested/loaded term: present it as critics' term alongside the neutral 'mandatory / pre-dispute arbitration.'

Attribute the critics' and defenders' views explicitly ('critics argue' / 'defenders argue'); the empirical claims (cost, speed, outcomes) are genuinely disputed — do NOT narrate them as settled fact.
Companies (AT&T, Epic Systems) named only for documented case facts; no editorializing.
kill_conditions: Cannot source the holdings and key facts to the primary opinions plus one independent reference.
